Optimal average power for Calcite cleaning without thermal damage
Near-IR wavelength for efficient Calcite absorption with minimal transmission
Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning resolution on Calcite surfaces

Typical range for ns-pulsed Nd:YAG lasers in stone cleaning applications; starting at 50W to avoid thermal damage to calcite (decomposition ~825°C). Max limited to prevent substrate ablation. Source: IPG Photonics YLR series specs and studies on carbonate cleaning (e.g., J. Cult. Herit. 2018).

Slower speeds for heavy contamination on delicate calcite; 500 mm/s optimal for balance of efficiency and minimal heat input. Range adjusts for light (faster) vs. heavy (slower) contaminants. Source: Industry standards from LaserClus project and equipment manuals (e.g., Adapt-Laser).

Nanosecond regime preferred for cost-effective cleaning with low reflectivity at 1064 nm; 10 ns minimizes heat-affected zone (HAZ) in low-thermal-conductivity calcite (~2 W/m·K). Picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) for ultra-precision but higher cost. Source: Laser physics reviews (Opt. Lasers Eng. 2015).

20 kHz balances cleaning speed and heat accumulation; higher rates increase efficiency but risk thermal buildup in calcite due to poor conductivity. Source: Academic literature on pulsed laser cleaning of minerals (Appl. Surf. Sci. 2012).

Fluence below calcite damage threshold (~2.5 J/cm² at 1064 nm ns pulses) for safe contaminant removal; 1 J/cm² recommended for industrial cleaning without ablation. Source: Ablation threshold studies (J. Phys. D: Appl. Phys. 2009).

200 μm for fine surface preparation on calcite; smaller for detail work, larger for broad areas. Matches typical galvo scanner setups. Source: Equipment specifications from Coherent and Trumpf laser systems.

Multi-pass (2 recommended) for complete removal without exceeding fluence limits; single pass for light contamination. Based on calcite's reflectivity (~5-10% at 1064 nm). Source: Industry case studies on stone restoration (Lasers in the Conservation of Artworks, 2017).
70% ensures uniform coverage and contaminant removal on irregular calcite surfaces; higher for heavy soiling. Source: Process optimization in laser scanning (Opt. Express 2014).
